data.title: John Neal's Previous Employer Lloyd's of London Reopened Inquiry Into Alleged Relationship, Sources — WSJ
AIG Withdrew Executive Hire After Inquiry into Alleged Inappropriate Workplace Relationship, Sources — WSJ
AIG Withdrew Executive Hire After Inquiry Into Alleged Inappropriate Workplace Relationship — WSJ
data.description:
By Jean Eaglesham and Emily Glazer

American International Group axed John Neal's appointment as the insurer's second-in-command after it discovered his previous employer launched an investigation into an alleged workplace affair, according to people familiar with the matter.

The insurance behemoth's last-minute reversal prompted a hasty reshuffle of senior AIG executives and stunned many in the industry. Insurance insiders asked how an appointment of an heir apparent could fall apart days before he was set to enter the building.

The stumbling block was Lloyd's of London, the world's largest insurance marketplace, which Neal helmed for more than six years before stepping down as CEO in January, the people familiar with the matter said.

The council that runs Lloyd's recently reopened an inquiry into Neal's conduct, the people said. It couldn't be learned what prompted the fresh inquiry or how AIG learned of it.

The episode raises fresh questions about AIG's vetting of top level appointments. It also puts a spotlight on the clubby insurance industry, dominated by a small circle of mostly male executives who vie for the top positions.

AIG is one of the world's largest insurers, with tentacles in close to 200 countries and more than 25,000 employees. It was rescued from near collapse in the 2008 financial crisis by one of the U.S. government's biggest ever bailouts.

The Lloyd's probe focuses on the nature of Neal's relationship with Rebekah Clement, until recently the director of corporate affairs at Lloyd's, the people familiar with the matter said.

In 2023, Neal promoted Clement to the newly created role, reporting directly to him. She sat on Lloyd's executive committee and she oversaw government policy, media relations and thought leadership, according to Lloyd's. She previously worked for the prime minister of New Zealand.

Her appointment was followed by the ouster of some senior staff in units she oversaw. She left the organization in May. Neal's successor as Lloyd's CEO didn't replace her.

Lloyd's employees complained to its governing council, akin to a board of directors, about the perceived preferential treatment of Clement, people familiar with Lloyds said. Concerns were also raised about the decision to employ Neal's daughter in the organization's press office, one of the people added.

Neal didn't respond to requests for comment. Clement didn't respond to a request for comment. Neal's daughter didn't respond to requests for comment.

Clement said her appointment followed a proper process, according to a person close to her.

A Lloyd's spokeswoman said its council commissioned an independent review in October after becoming "aware of market speculation concerning possible historic breaches of policy."

The review found Lloyd's "internal processes had not been fully adhered to in respect of a prior matter," the statement said. After new information emerged in recent days, Lloyd's launched an investigation with the support of a law firm, it added.

Before AIG announced his appointment in July, the company inquired with Lloyd's about Neal, one of the people familiar with the matter said. Lloyd's officials assured AIG that its governing council wasn't aware of any evidence of impropriety, the person added.

Neal had previously served as CEO of Australian insurer QBE Insurance Group. In 2017, QBE cut his bonus for failing to disclose a romantic relationship with his executive assistant.

"I think what was different in my personal situation was that I was the CEO," he told Financial News in 2020 about the QBE episode. He added: "And I felt that I had addressed what was a difficult situation internally as early as I could."

When Neal was looking for his post-Lloyd's role, he didn't have trouble finding opportunities despite the publicly disclosed QBE incident.

Neal initially accepted a job with Aon, the brokerage, before switching to the AIG role, seen as one of the top jobs in the industry.

Neal was set to earn up to $17 million in his first year as the powerful number two at AIG. The insurer halted his glide path into the role after learning this fall that Lloyd's council had reopened the probe of his conduct, people familiar with the matter said.

Lloyd's has long been abuzz about what people there saw as Neal's close relationship with Clement. They frequently traveled together, said the people familiar with Lloyd's.

AIG said in a securities filing on Nov. 14 that it had reached a mutual agreement with Neal that he would "no longer be joining the company due to personal circumstances."

AIG is still recovering from the exit of the executive who headed the unit Neal was meant to run. David McElroy left last year for what the company called "personal reasons."

He was later charged with four counts of felony sexual assault and lewd conduct that allegedly took place at an AIG retreat in Vermont. He pleaded not guilty and a lawyer for the executive has said his client "categorically denies the charges." A jury trial is scheduled for next year.

When Neal took over as Lloyd's CEO in 2018, he inherited a London marketplace steeped in a male-oriented, heavy-drinking culture dating back to its founding in the 1680s. Coverage by Bloomberg News and other media revealed mistreatment of women and bullying.

An independent survey Lloyd's published in 2019 found almost one in 10 of the market's participants had witnessed sexual harassment in the previous 12 months, most of whom felt uncomfortable reporting concerns.

Neal staked his reputation on a set of reforms to address concerns about sexual harassment and bullying within the Lloyd's marketplace. He led a ban on daytime drinking for Lloyd's direct employees and tough penalties for misconduct.

The Lloyd's market brings together hundreds of insurance buyers and sellers and has global influence on how companies and consumers protect themselves from catastrophe. It underwrites a swath of specialized risks, including for natural disasters, shipping, aviation and cyberattacks.

Write to Jean Eaglesham at Jean.Eaglesham@wsj.com and Emily Glazer at Emily.Glazer@wsj.com

data.title: EU wheat prices ease with Chicago as China-fuelled rally subsides
data.description:
Euronext wheat prices fell on Wednesday in step with Chicago as a grain market rally spurred by Chinese purchases of U.S. soybeans faded and wheat traders turned their attention back to ample global supply.

March wheat (BL2H6), the most-active contract on the Paris-based Euronext market, was down 1.4% at 193.25 euros ($222.95) by 1703 GMT, after reaching its highest in nearly two weeks at 196.50 euros.

December wheat (BL2Z5), less active following the expiry of options against the front-month position, was 0.5% lower at 189.75 euros after reaching a one-week top at 192.00 euros.

Chicago wheat slipped more than 1% in U.S. trading, retreating from a 4-1/2 month peak struck on Tuesday.

Wheat had drawn support from a rise in Chicago soybeans to a 17-month high this week as China stepped up purchases of U.S. beans following a trade truce between Beijing and Washington.

"The move up in wheat was overdone," a futures trader said. "There is plenty of wheat still there - if it's not Black Sea supply, it's Argentina, Australia and so on."

Underscoring ample availability of wheat, Ukraine's deputy economy minister told Reuters on Wednesday that the country would not restrict wheat exports in 2025/26 due to a higher harvest and slower shipments at the start of the season.

In Germany, traders reported low wheat selling by farmers who are more interested in providing more attractively-priced rapeseed and barley.

“It is difficult to get enough volumes of wheat together for export sales, while low prices offered for new-crop Argentine wheat are also providing hard competition especially in African markets,” one German trader said. “Black Sea prices are very close to EU levels but the Black Sea, especially Ukraine, is still getting purchase interest.”

Traders said a buyer in Sudan was seeking up to 70,000 tons of 11.5% protein Ukrainian wheat at around $260-$265 a ton cost and freight included (c&f) for January shipment, while a buyer in Libya was seeking around 30,000 tons of 12.5% protein Ukrainian wheat at $251-$253 a ton c&f for December shipment.

Feed barley supply remained tight, with Jordan getting no offers in a tender seeking 120,000 tons of feed barley on Wednesday.

Financial investors extended their net short position in Euronext wheat last week, data published by the exchange showed on Wednesday.

($1 = 0.8668 euros)

data.title: T.J. Maxx Owner Still Drawing Budget-Conscious Shoppers Ahead Of Holiday Season — Update
data.description:
By Connor Hart

T.J. Maxx parent TJX Cos. said it expects a strong holiday season as the off-price retailer continues to win over inflation-weary shoppers with its value and deals.

The company, which also owns Marshalls and Homegoods, on Wednesday once again lifted its full-year outlook after companywide comparable sales climbed 5% during the latest quarter. The increase, which beat both company guidance and Wall Street expectations, was driven by gains in store traffic and average spend per customer.

"Clearly, our value proposition continued to resonate with consumers," Chief Executive Ernie Herrman said on a call with analysts, adding that the company expects to continue to steal market share from competitors globally.

"As to the fourth quarter, we are off to a strong start," Herrman said.

TJX guided for same-store sales, or those from stores and digital channels operating for at least a year, to rise 2% to 3% during the current quarter, which includes the all-important holiday shopping season.

That outlook came in below the 3.1% that Wall Street forecast. However, JPMorgan analysts said they weren't concerned, noting that the past four quarters Herrman described as being "off to a strong start" all went on to post comparable sales at or above the high end of management's guidance.

William Blair analysts similarly cheered the company's latest report, saying it continues to outperform high expectations despite a volatile macroeconomic backdrop.

For the year, TJX now expects comparable sales to increase 4%, compared with a prior outlook of up 3%. The company additionally raised its earnings outlook to $4.63 to $4.66 a share, from $4.52 to $4.57 a share.

Wall Street is modeling same-store sales to climb 3.4% for the year, and for earnings of $4.61 a share.

The new outlook came as TJX logged higher profit and sales during the recent quarter. The company notched quarterly earnings of $1.28 a share for its three months ended Nov. 1, ahead of the $1.23 a share that analysts had forecast.

Sales jumped 7.5% to $15.12 billion and topped the $14.85 billion that Wall Street had projected.

data.title: Fitch Revises NFTA (Buffalo Niagara Int'l Airport) Rev Bonds Outlook to Positive; Affirms Rating
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-19 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority, NY's (NFTA) approximately $71.6 million of outstanding Buffalo Niagara International Airport (BUF) revenue bonds at 'A-'. The Rating Outlook has been revised to Positive from Stable.

RATING RATIONALE

The Positive Outlook reflects the airport's modest leverage and strengthening credit profile, in line with Fitch's indicative guidance for a higher rating, supported by growth in enplanements and net cash flows. Fitch could upgrade the rating if there is continued enplanement and revenue growth, leverage maintained below 3x, and execution of a successor airline use and lease agreement with at least as favorable cost recovery terms.

While Fitch expects the airport's passenger base to remain resilient amid broader political and macroeconomic conditions affecting U.S.-Canada cross-border traffic, given the moderate percentage of originating traffic from Canada, a sustained and material negative impact on enplanements that increases leverage above 3x could result in a return to a Stable Outlook.

The rating reflects the airport's modest enplanement base, its unique link to Canadian cross-border air travelers, manageable near-term capital investment needs with limited new debt expectations and a conservative debt structure with senior lien obligations. The airport maintains an established origination/destination (O&D) market position and diverse carrier mix of low-cost and national carriers.

As a "grandfathered" airport, BUF can make off-airport transfers to support other authority transportation operations. Although these transfers have the potential to reduce cash retention, they have not been made in recent years and management has no plans to resume such transfers. BUF's airline costs have remained steady but are somewhat high compared with regional airport peers.

KEY RATING DRIVERS Revenue Risk - Volume - High Midrange

O&D Airport with Exposure to Canadian Traffic

BUF is a medium hub airport with a predominantly O&D enplanement base, serving established markets across the Buffalo Niagara metropolitan statistical area (MSA) and the Southern Ontario region, as well as tourist demand from visitors to Niagara Falls.

The airport's proximity to the Canadian border, with competitive fares available at the airport, allows it to capture a significant amount of Canadian traffic. The airport is served by a diverse set of low-cost and national carriers, with no single airline accounting for more than a third of total enplanements.

Competition from Toronto's main airport, and to a lesser extent nearby Niagara Falls International Airport (IAG), which is controlled by NFTA, may pressure airline services at BUF and result in regional market share shifts. Airline costs have generally remained in the $13 per enplanement range, which Fitch views as competitive to peers, although somewhat higher than other airports in the region.

Revenue Risk - Price - Midrange

Compensatory Use and Lease Agreement

The most recent airline use and lease agreement (AUL) with the airport's signatory airlines expired on June 30, 2024. The airport is currently in a holdover status on a month-to-month basis with the same terms and conditions until BUF adopts a successor agreement, which is expected to be finalized in 2026. Fitch expects the successor agreement to have comparable cost recovery terms that do not have a material impact on the financial profile.

Terminal rates and landing fees are calculated using compensatory methodologies and, historically, provided favorable operating revenue to support strong debt coverage levels. While the base rate-setting approach limited airline payments to NFTA, additional protections were established through mid-year adjustments and an extraordinary coverage provision for signatory airlines to make up rate covenant deficiencies in the subsequent year. However, the airport can make off-airport transfers to support other authority transportation operations, although not expected, which could reduce cash retention and offset some of the stronger features of the AUL.

Infrastructure Dev. & Renewal - Stronger

Manageable Capital Plan

The airport's five-year capital improvement plan (CIP) through fiscal 2030 of approximately $135 million is manageable, with major projects consisting of passenger terminal improvements and general aviation apron reconstruction. The CIP is largely funded by grants, passenger facility charge (PFC) revenues, and cash. The airport may issue additional debt in the medium term to the extent future grant receipts and net surplus cashflow are insufficient to fund future capital needs.

Debt Structure - 1 - Stronger

Conservative Debt Structure

The airport's debt is 100% fixed rate and fully amortizing. Annual debt service requirements have a favorable profile, with future step-downs in stages through final maturity in 2039, dropping to $12 million in 2025 and below $5 million in 2030. Bond covenants and reserves are standard for U.S. airport credits.

Despite continued off-airport transfers to help subsidize IAG's deficits, off-airport transfers to other NFTA non-airport operations are capped, and liquidity remains adequate even when considering maximum allowable transfers. Liquidity, measured as unrestricted cash and operating reserves, has remained stable. The airport has operated with a historical 10-year average of over 300 days cash on hand (DCOH) and is expected to maintain over 200 DCOH through fiscal 2032 under the Fitch rating case, which assumes a lower balance in the airport development fund (ADF).

Financial Profile

BUF's financial profile improved in recent fiscal periods, with indenture-based debt service coverage ratio (DSCR), which includes PFCs and federal aid as a debt service offset, of 2.0x in fiscal 2025 and Fitch-calculated DSCR, which treats offsets as revenue, of 1.8x. A step-down in debt service supports higher Fitch rating case DSCRs, averaging 2.2x through fiscal 2032 when calculated with offsets as revenue. Leverage is currently low at 0.7x in fiscal 2025 and reaches net negative levels by fiscal 2029 in Fitch's rating case, but is projected to rise to 2.6x in fiscal 2031 as the airport undertakes additional borrowing for the CIP.

PEER GROUP

BUF's peer group includes regional airports with similar enplanement bases such as Rhode Island Airport Corporation (RIAC; A/Stable) and Long Beach (A-/Stable). RIAC and Long Beach both have modest traffic volumes and provide secondary service or face greater competition for air service from larger nearby airports in their respective markets than BUF, resulting in their 'midrange' volume risk assessments.

Long Beach has a considerably higher carrier concentration risk, while BUF and RIAC have a more modest concentration. Compared with Long Beach, BUF benefits from a lower forward leverage profile. Rhode Island benefits from no additional anticipated debt needs over the near term to support capital spending and unrestricted cash and reserves that exceed debt outstanding.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--A return to Stable Outlook may be warranted should financial performance lead to Fitch rating case leverage sustained between 3x and 5x;

--Higher-than-anticipated need for subsidizing IAG or other NFTA off-airport transfers, which materially weaken the airport's liquidity and financial flexibility;

--Further expansion to the CIP scope, resulting in increased borrowing needs that increase Fitch rating case total leverage above 5.0x on a sustained basis.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Execution of an airline agreement with as favorable cost recovery terms, coupled with sustained passenger growth, and Fitch rating case leverage stabilized below 3x. This compares to Fitch rating case leverage of 2.6x in fiscal 2031.

CREDIT UPDATE

BUF passenger activity remained steady in fiscal 2025 (ended March 31) and YTD fiscal 2026. Through the first six months of fiscal 2026, enplanements are up 1.3% from the same period in the prior year. While there has been a decline in the percentage of Canadian passengers originating at BUF in 2025, the downward trend appears to have reversed. The low point observed through the first half of calendar year (CY) 2025 was around 17%, rising to above 27% through September 2025, compared with the CY 2024 average of almost 35%. Management expects total passenger trends to remain steady in fiscal 2026, with annual growth of 1.1%.

In fiscal 2025, operating revenues grew by 12.5% compared with fiscal 2024, driven by growth in both airline and non-airline revenues, although slightly underperforming the Fitch base case by 3%. Non-airline revenues in fiscal 2025 increased primarily due to improving passenger recovery. Enplanements exceeded 2.5 million in fiscal 2025, increasing 8.8% YoY and outperforming Fitch's base case expectations by 0.8%. Operating expenses followed a similar trend to operating revenues due to increased business activity and rose 8.4% in fiscal 2025, below Fitch's base case expectations by 3.5%. Elevated costs were primarily due to increases in personnel and administrative costs, maintenance and repairs, and utilities.

The airport maintained healthy debt service coverage in fiscal 2025, well above its rate covenant of 1.25x, with support from a remaining balance of $3.9 million in federal relief funds. Fitch-calculated debt service coverage in fiscal 2025 that includes federal funds as revenues was 1.8x.

Like recent years, management has indicated that they have not budgeted for any off-airport transfers over the medium term but will continue to subsidize IAG's deficits with transfers from its ADF. Transfer amounts to IAG have historically been kept below $3 million. However, management has conservatively budgeted higher amounts in coming years.

Outside of the continued transfers to subsidize IAG's deficits, Fitch views the safe harbor amount that caps other off-airport transfers favorably, as well as adequate liquidity levels that remain when considering transfers at this maximum level. Furthermore, Fitch expects step-downs in debt service, together with management's demonstrated ability to maintain a prudent financial profile, provide adequate comfort regarding these transfer amounts.

FINANCIAL ANALYSIS

Both Fitch's base case and rating case assume the successor airline agreement will have comparable cost recovery terms. Fitch also assumes that NFTA issues $75 million of new money debt in fiscal 2030 to fund the CIP. However, Fitch expects potential debt needs to decline to the extent grants are secured and the airport uses pay-go funding.

The base case reflects the airport's fiscal projections for fiscal 2026 and enplanement projections through fiscal 2030. Enplanement growth thereafter is held steady at 1.0%. Non-airline revenues grow at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 2.2% over fiscal 2025-2032, generally tracking enplanements. Operating expenses from fiscal 2027 onwards grow at an annual rate of 3%.

Under these assumptions, Fitch-calculated DSCR, which treats PFCs as revenues, averages 2.4x through fiscal 2032. The indenture-based DSCR, which treats PFCs as offsets to debt service, averages 2.5x. Leverage is projected to decline to negative levels by fiscal 2028, but increase to 2.1x in fiscal 2031 following Fitch's assumed issuance of $75 million in new money debt.

Fitch's rating case assumes a hypothetical recessionary enplanement decline of 7% in fiscal 2027, followed by a 3.5% per annum recovery in fiscal 2028 and 2029. Enplanements thereafter grow at an annual rate of 1.0%. Non-airline revenues are expected to follow the growth rates of enplanements. Operating expenses are projected at 50bps higher than the base case, with some pass-through of higher expenses to airline rates. Under the rating case, Fitch-calculated and indenture-based DSCR average 2.2x and 2.4x, respectively. Like the base case, leverage is projected to reach negative levels by fiscal 2029, but increase to 2.6x in fiscal 2031, remaining consistent with a higher rating level.

SECURITY

The airport revenue bonds are supported by a pledge of net airport revenue generated by the authority from the BUF airport system. Any revenue derived from other airport facilities, including Niagara Falls International Airport and cargo operations at BUF, are not included in airport revenue. However, expenses or deficits related to those other airport facilities may be paid from net airport revenue deposited into the ADF.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. Climate Vulnerability Signals

The results of our Climate.VS screener did not indicate an elevated risk for Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority, NY.

ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: California refinery closures spark pipeline race to West Coast
data.description:
Key points:
  • Pipeline projects aim to fill void from refinery closures
  • Feasibility hinges on shipper commitments, analysts say
  • Projects face competition from seaborne imports

By Nicole Jao and Shariq Khan

A race is on among energy companies to build a major fuel pipeline to the U.S. West Coast, a potentially lucrative prize as the planned closure of two California refineries threatens to send gasoline prices in the isolated market soaring.

Motorists in West Coast states have long paid some of the country's highest fuel prices due to limited regional production and minimal connectivity to the Gulf Coast refining hub. There are no pipelines delivering fuel to California from across the Rocky Mountains and only a few pipelines deliver to the West Coast from the Gulf Coast, according to the Energy Information Administration.

Phillips 66's Los Angeles plant began winding down operations in September and Valero Energy's Benicia refinery plans to close in April, threatening more price shocks for consumers but presenting an opportunity for pipeline operators.

Three groups have outlined different proposals to fill the near 280,000 barrel-per-day supply void the closures create. These include refiner HF Sinclair, a unit of pipeline operator ONEOK , and a partnership between refiner Phillips 66 and midstream-focused Kinder Morgan .

However, the first to reach a final investment decision may be the only one to secure a potential multi-billion-dollar windfall because multiple pipelines to the West Coast would eat into each other's margins, which are already limited due to the availability of waterborne imports to California.

"When you see multiple pipeline projects being proposed at the same time, typically only one of them gets done," said Skip York, chief energy strategist at Turner, Mason & Co.

POLITICAL PRESSURE OPENS RARE WINDOW

The planned refinery closures have put intense pressure on California Governor Gavin Newsom to stop fuel prices from surging, creating a rare window for the approval of a fossil fuel project in a state that has long vilified "Big Oil".

"Given the backlash to refinery closures, it's hard to imagine much resistance to new projects," East Daley analyst Alec Gravelle said.

Capacity commitments make up most of the financing required to build pipelines, so securing at least 70% of the proposed projects' capacity could decide which of them progresses, York said.

That gives Western Gateway - the Phillips 66-Kinder Morgan project - and HF Sinclair's proposal an advantage, as the refiners themselves could guarantee some of the supply, Scotiabank analyst Paul Cheng said.

None of the proponents have yet announced any capacity commitments. Phillips 66 declined to comment on competing West Coast pipeline proposals. The other proponents did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

Proposals reusing existing lines also have a better chance of moving forward than new builds because regulatory approval may be easier, said Debnil Chowdhury, head of Americas and European refining at S&P Global Energy.

Parts of Western Gateway and HF Sinclair's plan propose using existing lines.

RIVAL REFINERS BET ON WATERBORNE FUELS

While a new pipeline could provide some stability to regional gasoline prices, refining executives have questioned whether any will ultimately get built, pointing to California's access to waterborne fuels.

"In terms of the pipelines that are rumored to come into the region, I would say that's a big if," said Rick Hessling, chief commercial officer for Marathon Petroleum, adding the timing and transportation cost of waterborne barrels trumped pipelines.

Valero Energy, the second-largest independent refiner, is unlikely to commit to a long-term shipping arrangement with any of the three projects, Chief Operating Officer Gary Simmons said during an investor call last month.

"We like the waterborne option because it allows you to source barrels from anywhere in the world and take advantage of international arbs that can be open," Simmons said, referring to price arbitrage opportunities.

data.title: NYMEX Overview: Petroleum Futures Back Off Steep Early Losses After EIA Data — OPIS
data.description:
Oil and refined product prices were lower heading into Wednesday afternoon but were backing away from earlier steep losses following the release of Energy Information Administration data showing a solid decline in U.S. crude inventories.

U.S. crude prices were down about 2.1% at 11:15 a.m. ET, with the December contract down $1.37 to $59.37/bbl in light trading ahead of expiration following Thursday's session. The more-active January contract was $1.47 in the red to $59.20/bbl, with both West Texas Intermediate contracts about 60cts above earlier lows.

January Brent crude was seeing losses of $1.40 to $63.49/bbl while February prices were down $1.42 to $62.99/bbl. Most activity in refined products was also focused on the next-month contracts.

The December ULSD contract, which had sunk as low as $2.5608/gal earlier in the day, was down 9.04cts to $2.6107/gal, while January prices were 8.6cts lower to $2.5387/gal.

RBOB futures were having a tougher time recovering, with December prices down 5.91cts to $1.9402/gal, a little over 1ct off its previous low of $1.9294/gal. January prices were 5.41cts in the red to $1.8679/gal.

Energy prices had started the day pressured by an inventory report released Tuesday evening by the American Petroleum Institute showing across-the-board builds in crude, gasoline and distillate inventories. Prices were also pressured by reports that the Trump administration had sent Pentagon officials to Ukraine in another effort to broker an end to fighting between that country and Russia.

EIA, however, on Wednesday morning reported U.S. crude inventories fell by 3.4 million bbl during the week ended Friday, leaving them about 5% below the seasonal five year average. EIA also reported a 2.3 million bbl build in

gasoline inventories and a 200,000 bbl increase in distillate supplies during the week. Despite the increases, gasoline remains 3% below seasonal averages while distillate supplies are 7% lower.

The declines came as EIA reported U.S. implied gasoline demand - as measured by products supplied - fell by 500,000 b/d to 8.528 million b/d. The decline was expected, as the first week of November normally sees a high demand reading.

Last week's reading was above both the same week last year and in 2023.

Distillate demand also fell, dropping by 136,000 b/d to 3.882 million b/d. This was also above the same week last year but down by more than 300,000 b/d from the same week in 2023.Gasoline and diesel prices were also moving lower in spot markets around the country, dragged down by the weakness in futures. Gulf Coast CBOB and RBOB, however, were seeing losses of more than 10cts/gal, as a cycle change overnight added additional headwinds to prices.

data.title: Healthcare's Momentum Gathers Steam. 3 Stocks With Bullish Charts. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Doug Busch

Healthcare stocks are recovering strongly as the year winds down. The Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF is the best-performing of 11 major S&P sector funds over the past week, month, and three-month periods.

The appeal of this group is its variety. It offers something for every type of investor. For the risk-averse, there are the defensive, mature pharma names. For the risk-seekers, young biotech stocks fit the bill. Medical equipment makers and managed-care providers also figure in.

Recent standouts have been large-cap biotech and pharma leaders: Gilead Sciences last week touched an all time high. Amgen is tracking a fourth straight weekly gain, which would mark its first such streak since January. I wrote about this in early October. Biogen is aiming for a fifth straight weekly advance, its first since late 2023. Eli Lilly has powered through the key $1000 level and continues to act well following its breakout above the double-bottom pivot at $864.45 from Nov. 3. Regeneron is looking for its third weekly gain in a row, after a 13% jump the week ending Oct. 13.

Let's take a look at a few more healthcare stocks with compelling chart setups.

HealthEquity, a health-information services play, is up 7% year to date and 14% over the past three months. While it sits about 12% below its 52-week high, it appears to be building the right side of a long, constructive base that began in early June. The pattern started with a bearish shooting-star candle on June 5, leading to declines in nine of the next 12 weeks. Recent action has improved notably, highlighted by a bullish golden cross as the 21-day exponential moving average pushed above the 50-day simple moving average. On Nov. 10, the stock broke forcefully above an ascending-triangle pivot at $98, rising 6.5% on heavy volume, and it is now distancing itself from the key $100 level. Look for a potential move toward $110 by year end and continued strength into 2026, while remaining constructive above $95.

Healthequity traded around $102 Wednesday.

Insmed, a biotech general, has gained nearly 200% over the last year and is a prime example of a strong name getting even stronger. The stock declined in consecutive weeks just three times this year. It is currently digesting its latest earnings reaction from Oct. 30, when shares jumped 16%, marking its first positive earnings move in the last five reports. The stock's real momentum began with a nearly 30% gap-up on June 10 that cleared a cup base, followed by a breakout above a cup-with-handle pivot of $105.45 on July 31, an action I highlighted this summer. Since then, it has almost doubled and is now attempting to push decisively away from the very round $200 level after breaking above a bull flag. Entries here look attractive, with a target of $235 by early 2026, while the bullish thesis remains intact above $185.

Insmed traded around $201 Wednesday.

Liquidia Corp, a biotech leader, is up nearly 200% over the last year and now trades just 2% below its 52-week high. The stock has showed powerful relative strength with an almost 8% gain this week heading into Wednesday versus just 1% for the SPDR S&P Biotech ETF. This week's advance follows two prior weekly gains of 7%. Since the early April lows, the stock has logged seven double-digit weekly gains, including four surges of more than 20%. Each of these closed at the top of their weekly ranges, a hallmark of strong institutional demand. Technically, the name continues to impress, breaking above a bull-flag pivot at the very round $20 level in early August and clearing a cup-base pivot of $30.04 on Tuesday. Look for a move toward $38 by early first quarter, and remain bullish as long as it holds above $27.

Liquidia traded around $30 Wednesday.

data.title: Forestar Group Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Forestar Group Inc., a national residential lot development company, has released its annual Form 10-K report, providing a comprehensive overview of its financial and operational performance for the fiscal year ended September 30, 2025. The report highlights the company's strategic initiatives, financial health, and the challenges it faces in the competitive real estate development industry.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenues: $1,662.4 million, reflecting a 10% increase from the prior year period, primarily due to an increase in average selling price per lot.
  • Cost of Sales: $1,298.9 million, increased due to higher revenues and elevated development costs.
  • Selling, General and Administrative Expense: $154.4 million, representing 9.3% of revenues, up from 7.9% in the previous year, primarily due to increased employee compensation and related costs.
  • Income before Income Taxes: $219.3 million, a decrease from $270.1 million in the prior year, impacted by higher SG&A expenses and a loss on extinguishment of debt.
  • Net Income: $167.9 million, down from $203.4 million in the previous year, reflecting the impact of increased costs and expenses.
  • Basic Net Income Per Common Share: $3.30, compared to $4.03 in the prior year, due to the decrease in net income.
  • Diluted Net Income Per Common Share: $3.29, compared to $4.00 in the prior year, reflecting the overall decrease in net income.

Business Highlights

  • Business Overview: Forestar Group Inc. is focused on land acquisition and development to sell finished single-family residential lots to homebuilders. The company operates in 64 markets across 23 states, providing a strong platform for consolidating market share in the fragmented lot development industry.
  • Geographical Performance: The company has expanded its operations across 64 markets in 23 states, which provides geographic diversification that lowers operational risks and enhances earnings potential by mitigating the effects of local and regional economic cycles.
  • Sales Units: For the year ended September 30, 2025, Forestar sold 14,240 lots with an average sales price of $108,400. The lot position at the end of the period consisted of 99,800 residential lots, with 65,100 owned and 34,700 controlled through purchase contracts.
  • Customer Segments: The primary customers are local, regional, and national homebuilders, with lots delivered mainly for entry-level, first-time move-up, and active adult homes. The company also markets some communities towards build-to-rent operators.
  • Operational Strategy: Forestar focuses on entitled short-duration projects that can be developed in phases, allowing for completion and sale of lots at a pace that matches market demand. This strategy aims to maximize capital efficiency and returns.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to remain disciplined in investing in land opportunities and managing lot sales pace and pricing to optimize returns. Forestar is well-positioned to consolidate market share due to its national footprint, strong local teams, and strategic relationship with D.R. Horton.
  • Operational Challenges: The company faces ongoing affordability constraints and cautious consumer sentiment, with homebuilders offering sales incentives to spur demand. Delays in municipal approvals are extending development cycle times in certain markets, and development costs remain elevated.
  • Strategic Relationship: As a majority-owned subsidiary of D.R. Horton, Forestar benefits from a strategic relationship that includes a Master Supply Agreement, providing D.R. Horton the right of first offer on lots from certain projects.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Market Consolidation: The company is focused on consolidating market share in the fragmented lot development industry by leveraging its national footprint, strong local teams, and strategic relationship with D.R. Horton. It plans to remain disciplined in land investments and manage lot sales pace and pricing to optimize returns.
  • Debt Management: The company issued $500 million of 6.5% senior notes due 2033 to fund a tender offer for its 3.85% senior notes due 2026, reflecting a strategic move to manage its debt profile.
  • Capital Management: The company maintains a strong liquidity position with $379.2 million in cash and cash equivalents and $588.9 million available under its revolving credit facility. It has no senior note maturities until fiscal 2028, and its debt to total capital ratio is 31.2%. The company has an effective shelf registration statement for $750 million of equity securities, with $300 million reserved for an at-the-market equity offering program. Additionally, the company has a $640 million senior unsecured revolving credit facility, which was increased to $665 million post-September 30, 2025.
  • Future Outlook: The company intends to maintain its net debt to total capital ratio at approximately 40% or less over the long term. It plans to continue managing its capital structure and liquidity profile to support growth objectives, with a focus on obtaining financing to fund expected growth. The company is also evaluating the impact of new accounting standards on its financial statements and disclosures, which may influence future capital management strategies.

Challenges and Risks

  • Competitive Industry: Forestar Group Inc. operates in a highly competitive and fragmented real estate development industry, facing competition from both larger entities with greater resources and smaller entities with lower cost structures. The company's strategic relationship with D.R. Horton, while beneficial, also presents risks related to concentrated ownership and potential conflicts of interest.
  • Economic Conditions: The company is subject to various risks, including those related to its concentrated ownership by D.R. Horton, which limits the influence of other shareholders and may lead to conflicts of interest. Economic conditions, such as inflation and interest rate fluctuations, pose significant risks to the homebuilding and lot development industries.
  • Operational Risks: Supply chain disruptions, labor shortages, and regulatory challenges, including environmental regulations and climate change-related policies, could adversely impact operations. Cybersecurity threats and compliance with evolving privacy and data protection laws also present significant risks.
  • Management Strategies: Management highlights ongoing affordability constraints and cautious consumer sentiment as challenges impacting new home demand. Despite these conditions, the company has increased revenues by focusing on affordable price points and leveraging its strategic relationship with D.R. Horton. However, delays in municipal approvals and elevated development costs remain challenges. The company plans to consolidate market share by maintaining a geographically diverse lot portfolio and managing lot sales pace and pricing to optimize returns.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including interest rate fluctuations that affect the cost of debt service obligations. The reliance on variable rate debt linked to the Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR) introduces additional financial risk. Changes in debt ratings could impact the company's ability to access capital markets or obtain credit on favorable terms.

data.title: Westlake Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
Fed's Miran Says Balance Sheet Could Eventually Shrink Further — Market Talk
data.description: Fed governor Stephen Miran says he supported the central bank's Oct. 29 decision to stop shrinking its balance sheet--and indeed would have preferred for the end to runoff to come a bit earlier than Dec. 1. But Miran says that in the future, the balance sheet could shrink more. In a speech Miran says that a web of bank regulations has artificially inflated the level of reserves that banks need to hold. If the Fed untangles that web--an effort that Fed vice chair for supervision Michelle Bowman has spearheaded--the Fed could then shrink its balance sheet further, which sucks reserves out of the system, Miran says. His view leans against the Fed's plan to begin buying bonds again in the future to match growing demand for Fed liabilities. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @matt.grossman)

data.title: Muddy waters' Carson Block says he is long Snowline Gold Corp
data.description:
Muddy Waters' founder Carson Block said on Wednesday he has taken a long position in Canadian gold miner Snowline Gold Corp , marking a departure for the famed short seller.

Snowline Gold Corp is involved with potential projects Block believes are undervalued compared to the price of gold and how much of the valuable metal the sites can produce, Block told attendees at the Sohn Conference in London.

Describing public reports the junior miner has released, Block said that he believed the company had taken a conservative approach to its own growth projections.

"We think this project works at $1,800 gold and don't see much downside risk here at the price," said Block.

Gold is traing at around $4,070 an ounce , not far from recent record highs.

The junior gold miner is located in the Yukon, Canada with multiple projects including the Rogue Valley Deposit which is reckoned to hold around 8 million ounces of gold.

The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

A long position is a essentially a bet that an asset will rise in value.

Canada Caught In a 'Vicious Circle' of Weak Productivity, Bank of Canada Official Says
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada is caught in a "vicious circle" of weak productivity that won't be broken without a regulatory overhaul to make the economy more conducive business investment, says a senior Bank of Canada official.

Deputy Governor Nicolas Vincent is the latest senior Bank of Canada policymaker to sound the alarm on the need to reverse Canada's decadeslong productivity slump, which he said is holding back wage growth and making the economy less resilient to shocks, like the abrupt shift in U.S. trade policy. He also made the link between improved productivity and keeping a lid on inflation.

"If we want to make things more affordable, we need to raise our income. And the way to grow our income is by increasing productivity." said Vincent, according to prepared remarks set for delivery in Quebec City.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Bank of Canada urges coordinated approach to boost weak productivity
data.description:
By David Ljunggren

OTTAWA, Nov 19 (Reuters) - The Bank of Canada on Wednesday called for a coordinated economy-wide approach to boost weak productivity, an issue it said was becoming more pressing in the face of challenges caused by U.S. trade policy.

The speech by Deputy Governor Nicolas Vincent was the latest effort by the central bank to urge policy makers to tackle decades of underperformance and insufficient investment.

"Shocks to our economy have become more frequent, and we are too vulnerable to their impacts," he said in a speech to Quebec economists.

"To put it bluntly, we're stuck in a vicious circle. When productivity is weak, it's much harder to meet current challenges and seize opportunities for the future."

Canada's productivity has been lagging for 25 years, he noted. Average annual growth of labor productivity was about 3% in the 1960s and 1970s but fell to 1% between 2000 and 2019 and is now below 0.5%.

Policy makers could help in three areas, he said:

* creating a better investment climate, in part by trimming a cumbersome and complex regulatory framework

* increasing competition in key sectors dominated by a few big players, such as telecommunications, passenger transportation and financial services

* investing in talent by spending more on training and making it easier to recognise the credentials of foreigners

"Weak productivity is a systemic problem that demands a coordinated approach across the economy ... given the tensions and conflicts that are destabilizing our economy, reversing the trend is more urgent than ever," he said.

Although Canadian businesses facing strong international competition are more productive, competitive and resilient, weak investment means too few firms fall into this category, he said.

When the Bank of Canada looked at factors driving inflation, the relationship between rising labor costs and productivity is particularly important, said Vincent. Stronger growth in productivity means incomes could rise while inflationary pressures remained limited, he added.

(Reporting by David ljunggren, editing by Dale Smith)

((Reuters Ottawa editorial; david.ljunggren@tr.com))

data.title: Adobe, Qualcomm partner with Humain on generative AI for Middle East
data.description:
By Stephen Nellis

Adobe and Qualcomm said on Wednesday they are partnering with Humain, the artificial intelligence firm backed by Saudi Arabia's sovereign wealth fund, to help the AI company develop tools for generating content in Arabic and for the broader Middle East.

The deal is one of several expected on Wednesday at a U.S.-Saudi investment forum being held as Saudi Arabian Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman visits Washington. Bin Salman on Tuesday met with U.S. President Donald Trump, who hosted a dinner with the crown prince and U.S. government and business leaders.

Adobe said it will integrate Allam, a large language model trained in Arabic, into its suite of apps used to create marketing campaigns, films and television shows. Humain will in turn use what the San Jose, California, company calls Adobe Foundry to "create tailored, generative AI models unique to the Arab world," according to an announcement from the firms.

The AI systems will run in data centers being developed by Humain and use chips from Qualcomm, which last month introduced new chips called the AI200 and AI250. Qualcomm's chips will handle the work of generating videos created by the models Humain is developing.

“We are building a new creative intelligence that understands our language, our values, our heritage, and our future with Adobe," Tareq Amin, CEO of Humain, said in a statement. "With the addition of Qualcomm into the collaboration, we will redefine the silicon that powers the next era of generative AI. Together, we are leading in developing creative AI for a new global era.”

data.title: Nvidia Shares Rise Ahead of Earnings; S&P 500 Pares Gains — WSJ
data.description:
By Rebecca Feng

Nvidia is due to report earnings late Wednesday, offering a snapshot of demand for chips that have been at the center of the artificial-intelligence boom.

The semiconductor maker's results will help investors gauge the validity of concerns about lofty valuations. Traders are positioning for a roughly 8% swing in the share price after earnings and through Friday, according to Cboe Global Markets data.

Nvidia was among the big tech stocks leading gains in morning trading, with shares up over 3%. Google parent Alphabet jumped nearly 5% and is poised to close at a record. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq composite drifted higher, but the Dow industrials turned lower, risking an extension of its four-session losing streak.

Lowe's shares rallied after the retailer's earnings showed its online business helped offset weakness in the home-improvement sector. Target and discounter TJX were among the other retailers posting results this morning.

In recent trading:

Among commodities, oil futures dropped more than 2.5%, while gold rallied.

Bitcoin prices held below $92,000.

Japanese bond yields rose, driven by expectations of a large economic stimulus package.
